text:
There was once an old oak tree that breathed life into barren fields. Birds sang silly songs around it. It loved stories. Well, it thought owl stories sounded pretty dumb.

One autumn evening, wind whispered secrets through leaves of oak. An old storyteller owl swooped down. Owls were famous story-spinners. This owl could weave tales with words.

"My dear forest," began the owl, "whispers told tonight will fill your branches tomorrow."

Together they spun tales. The old owl spoke of knights brave enough to slay giants. Of princesses charming hearts. Birds echoed fantasies.

Stars came out like jewels. Laughter rustled through rustles. Next morning dewdrops sang.

The oak didn't like knights. There were mice. Birds weren't funny. But owls? Owls could spin tales forever.

So it was. Tales came true—magical adventures. Birds didn't laugh. They watched and swooped.

The old oak grew old. Stars faded. But one
